description: Library of Intune Win32 app detection rule templates and requirement patterns for the Graph API

registry.description: Detect app by checking a registry key/value exists or has a specific value

templates.name: registry-exists
templates.description: Check if a registry value exists (any value)
templates.when: The installer creates a registry key/value as a side effect

templates.name: registry-integer-equal
templates.description: Check if a DWORD registry value equals a specific number
templates.when: The installer sets a DWORD value (e.g., Enabled=1, AllowTelemetry=1)

templates.name: registry-version-gte
templates.description: Check if a version string is >= a threshold
templates.when: The app writes its version to registry (common for MSI and some EXEs)

templates.name: registry-string-equal
templates.description: Check if a string registry value matches exactly
templates.when: The app writes a configuration string that confirms installation

templates.name: registry-uninstall-key
templates.description: Check Uninstall registry key (standard for MSI/EXE installers)

templates.name: registry-custom-marker
templates.description: Detection via a custom marker key written by a wrapper script
templates.when: No native detection artifact exists; wrapper script writes a marker after successful install

file.description: Detect app by checking if a file or folder exists, or a file version/size/date

templates.name: file-exists
templates.description: Check if a specific file exists at a known path
templates.when: The installer copies an EXE/DLL to a known location

templates.name: file-version-gte
templates.description: Check file version is >= a threshold
templates.when: Need to detect a specific version of an installed EXE/DLL

templates.name: folder-exists
templates.description: Check if an installation folder exists
templates.when: The installer creates a unique directory structure

templates.name: file-marker
templates.description: Check for a marker file created by a wrapper script
templates.when: Wrapper script creates a file to signal successful installation

productCode.description: Detect MSI app by ProductCode GUID

templates.name: msi-product-code
templates.description: Detect MSI installation by ProductCode and minimum version
templates.when: Package contains an MSI with extractable ProductCode

script.description: Detect app using a custom PowerShell detection script (exit code 0 = detected)
script.notes: Script must exit 0 if app is detected, non-zero if not. stdout is also evaluated.

templates.name: script-registry-check
templates.description: PowerShell script that checks a registry value with custom logic
templates.when: Detection requires complex logic (multiple conditions, value parsing)

templates.name: script-multi-condition
templates.description: PowerShell script that checks multiple conditions (AND logic)
templates.when: Detection requires verifying multiple artifacts together
templates.scriptTemplate:
$checks = @()
$checks += Test-Path 'C:\Program Files\<Product>\<app>.exe'
$checks += (Get-ItemPropertyValue -Path 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\<Vendor>\<Product>' -Name 'Version' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue) -ge '<MinVersion>'
if ($checks -notcontains $false) {
    Write-Output 'Detected'
    exit 0
}
exit 1

templates.name: script-service-check
templates.description: PowerShell script that checks if a Windows service exists
templates.when: The installer creates a Windows service

templates.name: script-config-validation
templates.description: PowerShell script that validates a configuration was applied correctly
templates.when: Package is a configuration script; detection must verify the outcome

guidance.x64: Most modern apps. Use when app has x64-specific binaries
guidance.arm64: ARM64 native apps (Surface Pro X, etc.)

customRequirements.name: aad-joined
customRequirements.description: Require device is Azure AD joined

detection: Always verify detection rules check OUTCOMES, not prerequisites
For MSI: ProductCode is the most reliable detection method
For EXE: file version check is more reliable than file-exists
For scripts: use a custom marker (registry or file) written by a wrapper
Never use generic OS keys like HKLM\Hardware
Test detection on both clean and already-configured machines
For 32-bit apps on 64-bit OS, set check32BitOn64System to true
requirements: Set minimum OS to the lowest version the app actually supports
Use 'all' architecture for scripts and interpreted apps
Add disk space requirements for large installations (>500MB)
Consider domain-join or AAD-join requirements for enterprise scripts
